## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Viva-Voce Cram Sheet):**

- ✅ **DC motor requires** both a *driver* (for current) and *PWM* (for speed) — **never** connect a motor directly to a microcontroller GPIO.
- ✅ **L293D pin numbers are mandatory** in every circuit diagram for KTU records — pin 1 = EN12, pin 2 = IN1, pin 3 = OUT1, pin 6 = OUT2, pin 7 = IN2, pin 8 = Vcc2, pin 16 = Vcc1.
- ✅ **Direction truth table:** `(1,0) = CW`, `(0,1) = CCW`, `(0,0) = brake`, `(1,1) = FORBIDDEN`.
- ✅ **PWM frequency sweet spot:** 1 kHz – 5 kHz. Too low → audible whine. Too high → switching losses overheat the L293D.
- ✅ **PWM duty-cycle formula:** $D = T_{ON} / T_{period}$, and $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$.
- ✅ **Match-register formula for any timer:** $M_R = D \cdot \frac{PCLK}{f_{PWM}}$.
- ✅ **Four GND pins of L293D (4, 5, 12, 13) must all be tied** to a common ground with the microcontroller and the motor supply.
- ✅ **Back-EMF and flyback diodes:** When the motor is suddenly switched off, the collapsing magnetic field induces a high-voltage spike. The L293D has *internal* clamp diodes for this — but if you use a discrete H-bridge, you **must add external flyback diodes** (1N4007).
- ✅ **Continuous current limit of L293D = 600 mA per channel.** If your motor draws more (e.g., a 1 A gear motor), use **L298** (2 A) or a **MOSFET-based driver** (BTS7960, up to 43 A).
- ✅ **PWM pin is connected to the ENABLE pin** of the L293D, not to the direction inputs. Direction inputs are *digital* GPIO.
- ✅ **Coast vs Brake:** Coast = EN = LOW (motor spins down slowly). Brake = IN1 = IN2 = same value (motor stops quickly due to internal short).
- ✅ **In the lab record**, always include: (i) aim, (ii) apparatus, (iii) **pin-out diagram**, (iv) **circuit diagram with pin numbers**, (v) **algorithm / flowchart**, (vi) **C program with comments**, (vii) **output observation table** (PWM %, motor speed in RPM, direction), (viii) **result and conclusion**.
- ✅ **Speed measurement in lab:** Use a **tachometer** (optical or mechanical) or count revolutions over a fixed time interval with a stopwatch.
- ✅ **Direction observation:** Mark a small white dot on the motor shaft with a marker pen — it makes the direction visually obvious.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interface a Stepper motor and rotate it in clockwise and anti-clockwise direction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAL308 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interface a Stepper motor and rotate it in clockwise and anti-clockwise direction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stepper Motor (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *stepper motor* is a **brushless, synchronous electric motor** that converts discrete electrical pulses—supplied by a microcontroller—into precise, discrete mechanical step rotations of its shaft. Unlike conventional DC motors that rotate continuously, a stepper motor's shaft rotates in **fixed angular increments called "step angles"** (commonly **1.8°**, **7.5°**, **15°**, or **18°** per step).

In the context of **PCRAL308 – Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems Lab**, the experiment requires a microcontroller (typically the **Intel 8051** family such as AT89C51/AT89S52 or P89V51RD2 used in KTU Kerala engineering colleges) to generate a precisely timed sequence of digital pulses. These pulses are delivered—via a current-driver/amplifier stage (such as the **ULN2003A** Darlington driver IC)—to the four stator windings (phases) of a **Unipolar Stepper Motor** (typically a **5-wire, 6-wire, or 8-wire motor rated at 5V/12V DC**, such as the **28BYJ-48**).

The KTU 2024 Scheme evaluates this experiment based on:
- **Hardware Interfacing** (40% weightage) – Circuit diagram, port connections, driver IC selection.
- **Embedded C Programming** (40% weightage) – Sequence generation, delay calculation, direction control.
- **Observation & Viva** (20% weightage) – Step angle verification, RPM calculation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Real-World Analogy – "The Four-Button Elevator":**
> Imagine an elevator that moves between floors only when you press a button. Each button corresponds to **one specific stator coil** of the stepper motor. The elevator (rotor) does NOT move continuously—it moves to the **next fixed position (step)** only when power is applied to the **correct coil in the correct sequence**. If you reverse the button-pressing order, the elevator moves in the opposite direction. This is *exactly* how a stepper motor works:
> - **Microcontroller** = The person pressing buttons
> - **Coils (A, B, C, D)** = Four elevator buttons
> - **Sequence (ABCD → BCDA → CDAB → DABC)** = The order of button presses
> - **Reverse Sequence (DCBA → CBAD → BADC → ADCB)** = Anticlockwise rotation
> - **Step Angle** = The fixed height between two floors

### 1.3 Why Stepper Motors are Critical in Embedded Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this experiment matters in KTU 2024 syllabus:**
> Stepper motors are the **backbone of precise motion control** without needing expensive closed-loop feedback (encoders). They are found in:
> - **3D Printers** (X, Y, Z axis control) — *uses 1.8° NEMA 17 steppers*
> - **CNC Machines & Robotics** (joint articulation)
> - **Hard Disk Drives** (head positioning)
> - **CCTV Cameras** (pan-tilt-zoom mechanisms)
> - **Medical Infusion Pumps** (precise drug delivery)
> - **Printers & Plotters**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Lab Motor Specification (28BYJ-48):**
> - **Rated Voltage:** 5V DC
> - **Step Angle:** **5.625°/64** (with internal gear reduction → effective **0.08789° per shaft step**)
> - **Gear Ratio:** **1:64**
> - **Phases:** 4 (Unipolar, 5-wire)
> - **Driver IC:** ULN2003A (used in KTU labs)

### 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition | KTU 2024 Significance |
|------|------------|----------------------|
| **Step Angle (β)** | The angular displacement per pulse | Determines positioning precision |
| **Step Resolution** | Steps per revolution (SPR) | $SPR = 360° / β$ |
| **Holding Torque** | Torque resisting shaft rotation when energized | Critical for static load support |
| **Detent Torque** | Residual torque when de-energized | Very low in 28BYJ-48 |
| **Full-Step Drive** | Only one phase ON at a time | Lower torque, low power |
| **Half-Step Drive** | Alternating single & dual phase ON | Higher resolution, smoother motion |
| **Wave Drive** | Single-phase excitation sequence | Simplest, used in basic KTU experiments |
| **Driver IC** | ULN2003A / L293D | Buffers microcontroller from motor's back-EMF |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — "The Magnetic Latch Mechanism"

A stepper motor operates on the principle of **Electromagnetic Attraction & Repulsion between stator and rotor teeth**. The rotor is a multi-toothed permanent magnet (or soft-iron core for VR-type), and the stator has **four electromagnets (A, B, C, D)** arranged around it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Magnetic Latch" Concept:**
> When current flows through a stator coil, it becomes an **electromagnet** with a definite North-South polarity. The rotor's permanent magnet aligns itself with this electromagnetic field (minimum reluctance position). When the next coil is energized, the rotor rotates to align with the *new* magnetic field—producing one precise "step." The microcontroller's job is to energize these coils in a specific *time-sequenced* order to produce continuous rotation.

### 2.2 Stepping Modes — The Three Standard Sequences

#### **Mode 1: Wave Drive (One-Phase-On) — Basic KTU Experiment**
- **Only ONE phase is energized at any instant.**
- **Power consumption:** Lowest
- **Torque:** Lowest (≈ 70% of full-step)
- **Steps per revolution:** $N_{steps}$

#### **Mode 2: Full-Step Drive (Two-Phase-On) — Improved Torque**
- **TWO adjacent phases are energized simultaneously.**
- **Power consumption:** Higher
- **Torque:** Highest (≈ 100%)
- **Steps per revolution:** $N_{steps}$ (same as wave drive)

#### **Mode 3: Half-Step Drive — Maximum Resolution**
- **Alternates between one-phase-on and two-phase-on.**
- **Steps per revolution:** $2 \times N_{steps}$
- **Smoother motion, finer positioning**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 Essential Formulas for KTU 2024 Lab Exam Calculations:**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Steps Per Revolution (SPR)** | $SPR = \dfrac{360°}{\beta}$ | dimensionless | $\beta$ = step angle in degrees |
| 2 | **Shaft Step Angle (geared motor)** | $\beta_{shaft} = \dfrac{\beta_{motor}}{G}$ | degrees | $G$ = gear ratio (e.g., 64 for 28BYJ-48) |
| 3 | **Pulse Frequency** | $f = \dfrac{RPM \times SPR}{60}$ | Hz (pulses/sec) | Determines motor speed |
| 4 | **Required Time Delay** | $T_d = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{60}{RPM \times SPR}$ | seconds | Time between two consecutive steps |
| 5 | **Rotational Speed (RPM)** | $RPM = \dfrac{60}{T_d \times SPR}$ | revolutions/min | Final shaft speed |
| 6 | **Total Steps for N revolutions** | $N_{total} = N \times SPR$ | steps | Used in loop counter |
| 7 | **Angular Position** | $\theta = N_{steps} \times \beta$ | degrees | Total angle rotated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Viva (Examiner's Favorite Questions):**
> - For **28BYJ-48** motor: $\beta = 5.625°$, $G = 64$
> - Therefore: $SPR_{motor} = 360° / 5.625° = 64$ steps
> - Therefore: $SPR_{shaft} = 64 \times 64 = 4096$ steps per revolution
> - Therefore: $\beta_{shaft} = 360° / 4096 = 0.08789°$ per step

### 2.4 Clockwise vs Anti-Clockwise — The Sequence Logic

> [!TIP]
> **🧭 The Direction-Reversal Trick (Very High-Yield for KTU):**
> To **reverse the rotation direction**, you simply **reverse the order of the energizing sequence**. The timing (delay) remains identical.

**Clockwise Sequence (C → CCW from rotor's perspective looking at the shaft):**
$$\text{CW} = \{(A,B,C,D),\ (B,C,D,A),\ (C,D,A,B),\ (D,A,B,C)\}$$
**Anti-Clockwise Sequence (reverse the CW order):**
$$\text{CCW} = \{(A,B,C,D),\ (D,C,B,A),\ (C,B,A,D),\ (B,A,D,C)\}$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial automation, stepper motors form the **open-loop positioning layer** of:
- **Pick-and-place machines** (PCB assembly)
- **Automated guided vehicles (AGVs)**
- **Bioprinters and textile machines**

The advantage of the **open-loop stepper** system used in this KTU experiment is its **simplicity, low cost, and zero feedback error accumulation when operated within torque limits**. The disadvantage is that if the load exceeds the holding torque, steps are *lost silently*—a critical consideration in real-world design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem Statement

> **Sample KTU Numerical:**
> *A stepper motor has a step angle of 7.5°. It is driven by a microcontroller in full-step wave mode. The motor is required to rotate the shaft at 15 RPM in the clockwise direction. Calculate: (a) Step Resolution (SPR), (b) Pulse Frequency, (c) Delay between steps, (d) Total number of pulses to complete 5 full revolutions.*

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Given Data:**
$$\beta = 7.5° \quad,\quad RPM = 15 \quad,\quad N = 5 \text{ revolutions} \quad,\quad \text{Mode} = \text{Wave Drive (Full-Step)}$$

---

**Step 1 — Compute the Step Resolution (SPR)**

By definition, the step resolution is the total number of discrete steps required to complete one full mechanical revolution (360°) of the motor shaft. This is the fundamental constant that governs all subsequent timing calculations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPR &= \frac{360°}{\beta} \\[6pt]
SPR &= \frac{360°}{7.5°} \\[6pt]
SPR &= 48 \text{ steps/revolution}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The shaft must advance **48 discrete increments** to complete one full circle. For 5 revolutions, this count will be multiplied by 5.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the Required Pulse Frequency**

The pulse frequency is the rate (in Hertz) at which the microcontroller must toggle the energizing sequence. Since one step corresponds to one pulse, the frequency is directly proportional to the desired RPM and the step resolution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{RPM \times SPR}{60} \\[6pt]
f &= \frac{15 \times 48}{60} \\[6pt]
f &= \frac{720}{60} \\[6pt]
f &= 12 \text{ Hz (pulses per second)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The microcontroller must output **12 complete stepping cycles per second** to maintain 15 RPM. Each cycle advances the rotor by exactly 7.5°.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the Delay Between Consecutive Steps**

The delay is the inverse of the frequency. This is the value that must be programmed into the software delay routine of the 8051 (typically using nested `for` loops or Timer interrupts).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= \frac{1}{f} \\[6pt]
T_d &= \frac{1}{12 \text{ Hz}} \\[6pt]
T_d &= 0.08333 \text{ seconds} \\[6pt]
T_d &= 83.33 \text{ milliseconds per step}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** After energizing each phase, the program must wait **83.33 ms** before moving to the next phase. This gives the rotor enough time to physically settle into the new magnetic alignment.

---

**Step 4 — Compute the Total Pulses for N Revolutions**

The total number of pulses (and hence the loop count in the C program) is simply the product of the number of revolutions and the steps per revolution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= N \times SPR \\[6pt]
N_{total} &= 5 \times 48 \\[6pt]
N_{total} &= 240 \text{ pulses}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The C program's outer `for` loop must iterate **240 times** to rotate the motor through 5 full clockwise revolutions. After every 48 iterations, the shaft returns to its original angular position.

---

**Step 5 — Cross-Verification via Time**

We can sanity-check the derivation by computing the total time taken for 5 revolutions and confirming it matches the expected wall-clock time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= N_{total} \times T_d \\[6pt]
T_{total} &= 240 \times 83.33 \text{ ms} \\[6pt]
T_{total} &= 19999.92 \text{ ms} \approx 20 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

And from the RPM definition:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{expected} &= \frac{N \times 60}{RPM} = \frac{5 \times 60}{15} = 20 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $\;T_{total} = T_{expected} = 20 \text{ seconds} \;$ ✅ **Derivation is consistent.**

---

**Step 6 — Anti-Clockwise Direction (Same Magnitude, Reversed Sequence)**

For anticlockwise rotation, **all numerical values (SPR, f, T_d, N_total) remain identical**. The *only* change is the **order of the phase-energizing sequence** inside the C program. This is the most elegant property of stepper motor control.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CW sequence index: } & i \rightarrow (i+1) \mod 4 \\[6pt]
\text{CCW sequence index: } & i \rightarrow (i+3) \mod 4 \equiv (i-1) \mod 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> **In Embedded C:** Replace `i = (i+1) % 4;` with `i = (i+3) % 4;` — and the motor reverses direction instantly.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Calculation Patterns

| KTU Calculation Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|----------------------|-------|------|-------------|
| Step Resolution | $\beta$ | $SPR$ | $SPR = 360°/\beta$ |
| Shaft Resolution (geared) | $\beta, G$ | $SPR_{shaft}$ | $SPR_{shaft} = (360°/\beta) \times G$ |
| Pulse Frequency | $RPM, SPR$ | $f$ | $f = (RPM \times SPR)/60$ |
| Time Delay | $f$ or $(RPM, SPR)$ | $T_d$ | $T_d = 1/f = 60/(RPM \times SPR)$ |
| Total Steps | $N, SPR$ | $N_{total}$ | $N_{total} = N \times SPR$ |
| Angular Displacement | $N_{steps}, \beta$ | $\theta$ | $\theta = N_{steps} \times \beta$ |
| Direction Reverse | — | Sequence order | Reverse the lookup table |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.3 Stepping Sequence State Diagram (Clockwise Flow)

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each state represents the energized phase pattern. The transition arrow represents one "step" (advancing by β degrees). Notice how the state `1100` (phases A and B energized) transitions through `0110`, `0011`, `1001` for the **Two-Phase-On Full-Step Drive**.

### 4.4 Software Flow — Program Execution Logic

### 4.5 Reference Embedded C Program (KEIL / 8051)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The code below is the KTU 2024 reference implementation.** The delay function uses nested loops calibrated for the standard 11.0592 MHz crystal used in KTU labs.

```c
#include <reg51.h>

/* Standard KTU 8051 Header - SFR Definitions for Port 1 */
#define MotorPort P1
#define TotalSteps 240     /* 5 revolutions x 48 steps/rev */
#define HalfSecond 500     /* Delay calibration constant */

/* Clockwise Wave-Drive Sequence Table (Active LOW for ULN2003) */
/* Phase Order: A -> B -> C -> D */
unsigned char code CW_Seq[4]  = {0xFE, 0xFD, 0xFB, 0xF7};
unsigned char code CCW_Seq[4] = {0xFE, 0xF7, 0xFB, 0xFD};

/* Software delay function - generates Td milliseconds */
void Delay_ms(unsigned int Td) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < Td; i++) {
        for(j = 0; j < HalfSecond; j++);
    }
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned int step;
    MotorPort = 0x00;        /* Initialize all coils OFF */
    Delay_ms(100);
    
    /* ---- CLOCKWISE ROTATION ---- */
    for(step = 0; step < TotalSteps; step++) {
        MotorPort = CW_Seq[step % 4];
        Delay_ms(83);        /* 83.33 ms for 15 RPM at 7.5 deg */
    }
    MotorPort = 0x00;
    Delay_ms(500);
    
    /* ---- ANTI-CLOCKWISE ROTATION ---- */
    for(step = 0; step < TotalSteps; step++) {
        MotorPort = CCW_Seq[step % 4];
        Delay_ms(83);
    }
    MotorPort = 0x00;
    
    while(1);  /* Halt - Infinite loop after rotation complete */
}
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define a stepper motor. List any two advantages of a stepper motor over a conventional DC motor.*

**Model Answer:**

A **stepper motor** is a brushless, synchronous electromagnetic device that converts a series of electrical pulses into discrete angular displacements (steps) of its rotor shaft. The rotation is directly proportional to the number of input pulses, and the speed is proportional to the frequency of the pulses.

**Two Advantages over DC Motor:**
1. **Precise Open-Loop Position Control** — No feedback encoder required. The shaft position is known *exactly* by counting the input pulses. A DC motor requires complex closed-loop PID control for the same precision.
2. **Excellent Holding Torque** — When the coils remain energized, the motor actively *locks* the shaft at the current position, providing inherent positional stability. A DC motor cannot hold position without active braking.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is the function of the ULN2003A driver IC in a stepper motor interfacing circuit? Why is it necessary when interfacing a stepper motor to an 8051 microcontroller?*

**Model Answer:**

The **ULN2003A** is a **high-voltage, high-current Darlington transistor array** containing seven independent NPN Darlington pairs. In a stepper motor interface, it acts as a **current amplifier and level shifter** between the low-power 8051 microcontroller and the relatively high-current stepper motor coils.

**Why it is necessary:**
- The 8051 port pin can source/sink only **~10 mA** of current, whereas a stepper motor coil (e.g., 28BYJ-48) typically requires **~200–500 mA** per phase.
- Connecting the motor *directly* to the 8051 pin would **damage the microcontroller** due to excessive current draw.
- The ULN2003A also provides **built-in flyback diodes** to protect against the back-EMF generated by the motor's inductive coils during switching.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU 2024 PART B Mark Distribution Pattern (Strictly Enforced):**
> - Part (a) carries **7 marks** — Theory / Diagram / Explanation
> - Part (b) carries **7 marks** — Calculation / Code / Tabulation
> - **No partial marks within sub-parts** — Step-wise presentation is MANDATORY
> - Use **diagrams, tables, and flowcharts** to secure full marks (Valuator reference)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Theory + Code Dominant

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of a **unipolar stepper motor** with the **8051 microcontroller** using the **ULN2003A** driver IC. Clearly specify the port pins used, the role of the driver, and the four phase-coil connections. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an **embedded C program** for 8051 to rotate the stepper motor **clockwise for 5 full revolutions** and then **anti-clockwise for 5 full revolutions** in wave-drive mode. Assume the stepper motor has a step angle of **7.5°** and the required shaft speed is **10 RPM**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A:

**Solution to Part (a):**

The block diagram consists of three primary subsystems interconnected as follows:

| Subsystem | Component | Function |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Controller | AT89C51/52 (8051 family) | Generates the 4-bit phase sequence on Port P1 (P1.0 to P1.3) |
| Driver | ULN2003A (Darlington Array) | Boosts current from mA-level to A-level for the motor coils |
| Actuator | 28BYJ-48 Unipolar Stepper Motor | Converts electrical pulses to mechanical rotation |

**Port Pin Mapping:**

| 8051 Pin | ULN2003A Input | ULN2003A Output | Motor Coil | Wire Color |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|------------|------------|
| P1.0 | IN1 (Pin 1) | OUT1 (Pin 16) | Coil A | Orange |
| P1.1 | IN2 (Pin 2) | OUT2 (Pin 15) | Coil B | Yellow |
| P1.2 | IN3 (Pin 3) | OUT3 (Pin 14) | Coil C | Pink |
| P1.3 | IN4 (Pin 4) | OUT4 (Pin 13) | Coil D | Blue |
| GND (Pin 20) | GND (Pin 8) | — | — | — |
| +5V External | VCC (Pin 9) | — | Common (Red) | Red |

> **Note:** The common (red) wire of the 28BYJ-48 is connected to an **external +5V supply**, NOT to the 8051. The ULN2003A outputs *sink* current to GND when the corresponding input is HIGH (active-low logic for this configuration).

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the required delay:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPR &= \frac{360°}{7.5°} = 48 \text{ steps/rev} \\[6pt]
T_d &= \frac{60}{RPM \times SPR} = \frac{60}{10 \times 48} = 0.125 \text{ s} = 125 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total steps for 5 revolutions:**
$$N_{total} = 5 \times 48 = 240 \text{ steps}$$

**Step 3 — Embed the C Code:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

#define Motor P1
#define TotalSteps 240
#define DelayConst 125

unsigned char code CW[4]  = {0xFE, 0xFD, 0xFB, 0xF7};
unsigned char code CCW[4] = {0xFE, 0xF7, 0xFB, 0xFD};

void Delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i=0; i<ms; i++)
        for(j=0; j<1275; j++);  /* Calibrated for 11.0592 MHz crystal */
}

void Rotate(unsigned char direction, unsigned int steps) {
    unsigned int i;
    for(i = 0; i < steps; i++) {
        Motor = direction[i % 4];
        Delay_ms(DelayConst);
    }
}

void main(void) {
    Motor = 0x00;
    Delay_ms(100);
    Rotate(CW, TotalSteps);     /* Clockwise: 5 revolutions */
    Delay_ms(500);              /* Pause at end */
    Rotate(CCW, TotalSteps);    /* Anti-clockwise: 5 revolutions */
    Motor = 0x00;
    while(1);
}
```

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Numerical + Diagram Dominant

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Module 2]**

**(a)** A stepper motor has a **step angle of 1.8°** and is driven by an 8051 in **full-step, two-phase-on mode**. Compute the following: **(i)** Steps per revolution, **(ii)** The pulse frequency required to achieve **120 RPM**, **(iii)** The software delay between consecutive steps in milliseconds, **(iv)** The total number of pulses to rotate the shaft by **540°**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the **state transition table** for both **clockwise and anti-clockwise** wave-drive sequences of a 4-phase stepper motor. Show the binary output pattern sent to Port P1 for each step and explain the direction-reversal logic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B:

**Solution to Part (a):**

**(i) Steps Per Revolution (SPR):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
SPR &= \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/revolution}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Pulse Frequency for 120 RPM:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{RPM \times SPR}{60} = \frac{120 \times 200}{60} = 400 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Software Delay:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{400} = 0.0025 \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Total Pulses for 540°:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{pulses} &= \frac{540°}{1.8°/\text{step}} = 300 \text{ pulses}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Cross-check:** 300 pulses × 2.5 ms/pulse = 750 ms = 0.75 s. Expected: 540°/360° × (60s/120) = 0.75 s. ✅ Consistent.

**Solution to Part (b):**

**State Transition Table for 4-Phase Stepper Motor (Active-Low Drive via ULN2003):**

| Step # | Phase A (P1.0) | Phase B (P1.1) | Phase C (P1.2) | Phase D (P1.3) | Hex Value | CW Direction | CCW Direction |
|:------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:---------:|:------------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | OFF | 0xFE | ✓ | ✓ |
| 2 | OFF | ON | OFF | OFF | 0xFD | ✓ | |
| 3 | OFF | OFF | ON | OFF | 0xFB | ✓ | |
| 4 | OFF | OFF | OFF | ON | 0xF7 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 5 | ON | OFF | OFF | OFF | 0xFE | ✓ | |
| 6 | OFF | ON | OFF | OFF | 0xFD | | ✓ |
| 7 | OFF | OFF | ON | OFF | 0xFB | | ✓ |
| 8 | OFF | OFF | OFF | ON | 0xF7 | | ✓ |

**Direction-Reversal Logic:**

> [!TIP]
> **The Elegant Direction Trick:** The CW sequence moves *forward* through the array index (0 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 0...), while the CCW sequence moves *backward* (0 → 3 → 2 → 1 → 0...). This is achieved in code by:
> - **CW:** `array[i % 4]` where `i` increments normally
> - **CCW:** `array[(4 - (i % 4)) % 4]` OR pre-store the reverse-ordered array
> - **C Equivalent:** Replace `i++` with `i = (i + 3) % 4` (modular decrement)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to De-energize:** After the rotation completes, students often leave the last coil energized. This *wastes power* and *heats up the motor*. Always end with `MotorPort = 0x00;`
> 2. **Confusing Active-High vs Active-Low:** The ULN2003A inverts the signal. A logical `1` on the 8051 pin turns the corresponding coil **ON**. Do not use `0x01` for Phase A — use `0xFE` (all bits HIGH except LSB).
> 3. **Wrong Delay Calculation:** Using `SPR = 200` (motor step) instead of `4096` (shaft step) for the **28BYJ-48 geared motor** is a classic mistake. Always clarify whether the question refers to the **motor's internal step** or the **output shaft's effective step**.
> 4. **Missing `while(1)`:** The program must NOT exit `main()` and restart. Add `while(1);` at the end to halt execution after rotation.
> 5. **Power Supply Mistake:** Forgetting to power the stepper motor's **common (red) wire** with an **external 5V supply**. The 8051's 5V pin can supply only ~100 mA — insufficient for the motor.
> 6. **No Flyback Protection:** Direct connection without ULN2003A can damage the 8051 due to back-EMF spikes. Mention the role of the **internal flyback diodes** in ULN2003A.

### 5.3 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 Important Things To Remember (Print This & Stick to Your Wall!):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Stepper motor = **brushless, synchronous motor** that rotates in **discrete steps**, not continuously.
- ✅ **Driver IC:** **ULN2003A** is mandatory in KTU labs — never connect motor directly to 8051.
- ✅ **Port Used:** **Port P1** (P1.0–P1.3) is the standard 4-bit interface.
- ✅ **Step Resolution:** $SPR = 360° / \beta$ (e.g., 7.5° motor → 48 steps/rev).
- ✅ **Geared Motor (28BYJ-48):** Internal step = 5.625°, Gear ratio = 64:1, **Effective shaft step = 0.08789°** → 4096 steps/rev.
- ✅ **Delay Formula:** $T_d = 60 / (RPM \times SPR)$ seconds.
- ✅ **Direction Control:** **Reverse the sequence table** — timing remains identical.
- ✅ **Modes:** **Wave Drive** (1-phase) → low torque; **Full-Step** (2-phase) → high torque; **Half-Step** → 2× resolution.
- ✅ **Active Logic:** ULN2003A is **inverting** — write `0xFE`, `0xFD`, `0xFB`, `0xF7` (NOT `0x01`, `0x02`, `0x04`, `0x08`).
- ✅ **Common Wire (Red):** Connect to **external +5V**, NOT to 8051.
- ✅ **End-of-Rotation:** Always de-energize coils with `MotorPort = 0x00;` and halt with `while(1);`.
- ✅ **Crystal Frequency:** 11.0592 MHz is the KTU standard — calibrate delay loops accordingly.
- ✅ **Half-Step Advantage:** Doubles resolution, smoothens motion, reduces resonance.
- ✅ **Holding Torque:** Maximum torque the motor can resist *without losing steps* when energized.
- ✅ **Detent Torque:** Residual (passive) holding torque when de-energized.
- ✅ **Back-EMF Protection:** ULN2003A has built-in **free-wheeling diodes** — do not bypass them.

> **🎯 One-Line Exam Mantra:** *"Reversing the sequence reverses the direction; the delay is your speed knob."*
